metadata.title: Headline, Visual, Natural Tool Mention, CTA — Then Publish
metadata.playlist: Build a Home — Thought Leadership on Substack
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 10 brief — Self-Promotion Through Thought Leadership

beats.narration_text: Hola — this is Liam. Episode 4 of 5. The draft from Episode 3 is a great article that nobody has read yet. This episode finishes it: a headline that earns the click, a visual that carries the brand, the tool mention checked for naturalness, a clear CTA, and then the live publish. The headline is the most underweighted part of most student submissions. Everything else can be excellent and the wrong headline means nobody gets there. Let's go.

props.segment: Headline, Visual, Natural Tool Mention, CTA — Then Publish
props.command: Headline (read-aloud tested) + custom visual (on-brand) + tool mention (natural) + CTA → publish live.
props.runningText: headline is the most underweighted element — write 3, test them aloud, pick the one you'd click…
output: 1 custom visual (tool screenshot / branded graphic / process diagram)

beats.act: the headline — would you click it
beats.narration_text: The headline test: read it out loud and ask yourself 'would I click this?' — the same gut-check honesty as the UVP from Assignment 6. The skep:subject skill generates three headline options from the thesis and the hook. The characteristics of a headline that earns a click: it is specific, not clever. Not 'The Future of AI' — 'Why Most AI Pipelines Fail Quietly and How to Find Out if Yours Is.' It implies the reader will get something — a framework, a diagnosis, a specific answer. It matches the article's actual content — a headline that promises a list article on top of a narrative article is a lie and the reader will leave when they notice. It avoids filler words: 'Everything You Need to Know,' 'The Ultimate Guide,' 'Why You Should.' Write three headline options before picking one. For each: the type — promise, counterintuitive, question, or how-to — and a one-sentence test of whether you would click it. The one you pick goes at the top of the Substack post. The other two go in the Your Turn notes for future articles. A headline you would not click yourself is a headline that will not get clicked.

props.artifactTitle: The headline — would you click it?
props.artifactHeading: Write 3 options. Pick 1. Read aloud first.
artifactLines: Characteristics: specific (not clever) · implies a gain · matches actual content · no filler.
Filler to avoid: 'Everything You Need to Know,' 'Ultimate Guide,' 'Why You Should.'
4 headline types: promise · counterintuitive · question · how-to.
Write 3 options → per option: type + 'would you click this?' test.
skep:subject generates 3 from your thesis + hook → pick the one you'd click.
props.sparkLine: Headline you wouldn't click yourself = headline that won't get clicked. Write 3 first.

beats.act: the visual + the natural tool mention
beats.narration_text: The visual. At least one custom visual — the same requirement as Assignment 9, with the same test: if you swapped the text on this image, could it be about anything? If yes, it is generic. The best visual options for a thought-leadership article: a screenshot of the Madison tool's output — the actual thing the tool produces — is the most credible option, because it is evidence rather than illustration. A branded graphic using the publication's palette with a pull quote from the article. A process diagram that visualizes the framework you built the article around. A data visualization if the article includes any numbers. Canva, Figma, and Adobe Express are all free options. Use your brand assets from A7 and A8 — same palette, same font treatment. Size: twelve hundred by six hundred pixels for Substack. Embed it at the top of the article before the first paragraph. The natural tool mention. Open the draft and find every sentence that mentions the Madison tool. For each one, ask: does the tool appear here because it adds something to the argument at this point, or does it appear here because I want the reader to know about it? If the answer is the second one, the sentence needs to be rewritten so the tool adds value to the argument at that point — or moved to a place in the article where it does.

props.artifactHeading: Custom visual · tool mention that adds to the argument
artifactLines: Visual types: tool output screenshot · branded pull quote · process diagram · data viz.
Generic test: swap the text — could it be about anything? If yes, redo it.
Size: 1200×600px for Substack. A7/A8 palette + font. Embed before first paragraph.
Tool mention check: for each mention, ask: 'does the tool add to the argument HERE?'
If the answer is 'I want the reader to know about it' → rewrite or move it.
props.sparkLine: Tool screenshot = evidence. Natural mention = adds to the argument at that point.

beats.narration_text: The CTA. Same three types as Assignment 9, now adapted for a Substack article. The subscribe CTA: 'If this was useful, subscribe — I publish once a week on what I am building and learning.' The connect CTA: 'If you are working on the same problem, I would like to compare notes — connect with me on LinkedIn.' The try-it CTA: 'The tool is available at [URL] — the first run is free and the quality gate criteria are fully configurable.' One thing different from the A9 article: a Substack article should also have a subscribe button visible on the page, which Substack adds automatically. But the written CTA at the end of the article is still required — do not rely on the subscribe button alone. Then publish. Click Publish in Substack, set it to free and public. Copy the URL. Log out of your account and check the URL in an incognito window — same check as A9. The URL must open the article for someone who has never visited the publication. That URL goes in the PDF submission and in the LinkedIn promotion post. Published and imperfect beats perfect and invisible. The eighty-twenty rule: the skill is earning attention you were never promised — do not polish into paralysis. Ship it.

props.artifactHeading: Subscribe · Connect · Try it · Then ship
artifactLines: CTA types: subscribe (once/week, what you build/learn) · connect (same problem → compare notes) · try it (tool URL + what it does).
Do not rely on Substack's subscribe button alone — write the CTA sentence too.
Publish: free + public. Copy URL. Check in incognito — publicly visible or not done.
URL goes in the PDF + LinkedIn promo post.
80/20: published and imperfect beats perfect and invisible. Ship it.
props.sparkLine: Free + public + incognito check + URL copied. Then it's live. Ship it.

beats.narration_text: Verdict for Headline, Visual, Natural Tool Mention, and CTA. Three headline options, one chosen after the read-aloud gut-check — specific, implies a gain, matches content, no filler. One custom visual using brand assets from A7 and A8 — tool screenshot, branded pull quote, process diagram, or data viz — generic test applied. Tool mentions checked: each one adds to the argument at that point, or it is moved or rewritten. CTA: subscribe, connect, or try it — plus the written sentence, not just the Substack subscribe button. Publish: free and public, copy URL, check in incognito. Published and imperfect beats perfect and invisible. Episode 5 is the promo post, the reflection, and the final submission.

props.artifactTitle: Headline, Visual, Natural Tool Mention, CTA — Then Publish
artifactLines: Headline: 3 options, 1 chosen (read-aloud tested) — specific, gain implied, matches content, no filler.
Visual: custom, on-brand, A7/A8 assets, generic test applied. 1200×600px.
Tool mention: adds to argument at that point — not an announcement.
CTA: written sentence (subscribe/connect/try it) + Substack subscribe button.
Publish: free + public + incognito check + URL copied. Published imperfect > invisible.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. The headline prompt. Paste this into Claude or skep:subject after your draft is ready. The prompt reads: I need three headline options for my thought-leadership article. My thesis is: [paste it]. My hook type was: [counterintuitive / specific moment / question]. The article's main transferable framework or insight is: [one to two sentences]. The primary reader is: [describe who this is written for]. Write three headline options — one promise headline that tells the reader exactly what they will get; one counterintuitive headline that states the unexpected claim from the article's thesis; one question headline that asks the question the reader did not know they had. For each headline: the type, the headline text, and one sentence answering 'would you click this if you were the primary reader I described?' Avoid: 'everything you need to know,' 'ultimate guide,' 'why you should,' and any phrase that could describe a different article. After you choose the headline, read the chosen one out loud one more time. If you hesitate, write a fourth option. The headline is the last thing to finalize before you click Publish.

props.segment: Headline, Visual, Natural Tool Mention, CTA — Then Publish
props.command: Thesis: [paste]. Hook type: [choose]. Framework: [1–2 sentences]. Primary reader: [describe]. Write 3 headlines: promise · counterintuitive · question. Per headline: type + text + 'would primary reader click this?' Avoid filler.
props.runningText: headline is the last thing before Publish — read it aloud one more time, if you hesitate write a 4th…

beats.narration_text: Headline, Visual, Natural Tool Mention, CTA — Then Publish. Liam, in for Bear.
